comments.text: So like, Canadians, how much are we thinking per ship trying to transit out of Seattle to the Pacific or Great Lakes to the Atlantic? Not too much to start until we get AShM batteries and OWA drones online.

Nov. 30, 1829:
The Welland Canal officially opened as two ships passed from Port Dalhousie to Port Robinson, Upper Canada. The Canal linked Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. The canal would be expanded several times. Learn more 👇

Canada built a world-class system of canals and locks to get ships from the Great Lakes to tidewater, just in time for railroads to take over. Then, we neglected our railroads in favour of highways, which are now so clogged that we're revisiting the value of ships. There's a lesson here, methinks.

What we do, Love Canal, located in Niagara Falls, New York was a hopeful project to connect Lake Erie to Lake Ontario (1890’s). However, the project faultered and only 1 mile of the canal was dug. Later the canal was used as a dumping ground for the local municipality.

The town where my grandparents’ used to live has the St. Lawrence Seaway running through it (canal that ships use to get between Lake Erie and Lake Ontario, they can’t use the Niagara River because of the Falls), and today a ship scraped the side of one of the bridges. It’s been closed all day. 😭

a thing to keep in mind about turn of the century railways was a surprisingly large amount of the geography didn't actually make much if any money. The money was made in handling prairie grain from the farms to Thunder Bay (Lake Superior) and in immigrants and manufactured goods out to the farms
